Advanced stats

NBA-style deep metrics, format-normalized so 21-point and 5-point tournaments compare fairly. Eligibility scales to the active season.

Offense & defense
Share of points won/lost per match — format-normalized so 21-point and 5-point tournaments combine fairly. 60% points share means you average 60% of all points played in your matches.

Highest share of points won

Lowest share of points allowed

Clutch performance
Record in close games — matches decided by ≤15% of total points (works across both scoring formats)
Streaks
Longest consecutive wins and losses across chronological match history

Longest win streaks

Longest loss streaks

Dominant matches
Matches decided by ≥80% of total points (format-agnostic blowouts) — won and suffered

Most dominant wins

Most dominant losses

Strength of schedule
Average win rate of opponents faced — who played the toughest competition
Hidden value
Positive points-share advantage despite ≤55% win rate — players who win their matches by larger margins than they lose them
Match-timing splits
First match vs final match of each tournament — fast starters vs late finishers

Fast starters · first-match win rate

Late finishers · final-match win rate

Quality wins vs bad losses
Wins over above-average opponents (≥55% wr) vs losses to below-average (≤45% wr)

Head-to-head

When player A and player B were on opposite teams. "A wins" = how often A's team beat B's team.

Data notes

Methodology and caveats

Two scoring formats — and how we normalize

The tournaments use two scoring systems. 21-point format (55 tournaments): race to 11, can go up to a 21-point total — typical scores like 13-8, 16-5, 11-10. 5-point format (84 tournaments): first to 4, 5 if tied — typical scores like 3-1, 4-0, 4-3.

Raw "points per match" would be meaningless across these — averaging an 11-point match with a 4-point match gives a garbage number. So offense/defense and other point-based stats use points share: the percentage of total points you won in each match, averaged across all your matches. A 13-8 win = 62% share. A 3-1 win = 75% share. Both formats then compare fairly.

Similarly: dominant matches = won/lost with ≥80% of points (catches 4-0 in B and 17-4 in A — any format-appropriate blowout). Close games (clutch) = decided by ≤15% of total points (catches 11-10 and 4-3, but excludes 3-2 since 5 total points isn't really tight).

Chemistry — how it works

Each player has an individual career win rate in the active season — what % of matches they won. For a partnership, we compare their actual pair win rate vs the average of their two solo win rates. The difference is chemistry.

Example: if Foe wins 51% of his matches alone, Iyo wins 53%. Average = 52%. As partners they went 24-5 (83%). Chemistry = +31% — they play dramatically better together than apart.

Reliability scales with sample size. A 5-match partnership at 100% win rate could just be a lucky streak. The default filters are tuned per-season: all-time and 2025 use ≥10 matches together, 2026 (smaller sample) uses ≥6.

Data source & coverage

140 tournament result pages were fetched from americano-padel.com/print/... — the print endpoint serves the complete match log in plain HTML. Every played match across every tournament from April 2025 to May 2026 is in this dataset: 2,707 played matches total. (837 additional matches were scheduled but never played.)

Player toplists (W-L-Diff-Points columns from the result page) aren't on the print endpoint, so I recompute them from the match results: rank = sort by (wins desc, point differential desc, games scored desc). This may differ slightly from the official toplist if the app uses a different tiebreaker.

11 additional tournaments from app.pdlup.com were in the source CSV but couldn't be fetched — pdlup is a client-side JS app that doesn't render server-side. They are not in this analysis.

3 tournaments use a fixed-pair format (preset partnerships that don't shuffle): madre fixed, Madre (Nov 13), Fix Orange. In these the player names were entered as combined pair strings ("Roby andreas"); I split them on whitespace into individual players so the chemistry analysis stays consistent.

Likely name variants — review needed

Players entered their names freely so the same person may appear under multiple spellings. Each variant is treated as a distinct player here. Below are pairs with similarity ≥ 75% (same starting letter) — review and merge as you see fit.
